questions.prompt: Ask in this order:
Was this an adult case or juvenile case?
Was it a conviction, dismissal, acquittal, no-charge arrest, diversion/deferred prosecution, or still pending?
What court handled it: district, municipal, superior, or juvenile?
What county and case number?
What was the exact charge and RCW/statute?
Was it a misdemeanor, gross misdemeanor, Class B felony, Class C felony, or something else?
When were you sentenced?
When were you released from jail/prison/confinement?
When did probation/supervision/community custody end?
Are all sentence terms complete, including treatment and required obligations?
Do you owe restitution to an individual victim?
Do you have any pending charges in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court?
Have you had any new convictions in the relevant 3-, 5-, or 10-year period?
Was the case DUI, physical control, reckless/negligent driving connected to alcohol/drugs, or another prior offense?
Was the offense domestic violence, sex-related, violent, crime against persons, child exploitation, obscenity/pornography, firearm/deadly weapon-enhanced, or sexually motivated?
Was this conviction connected to trafficking, sexual assault, domestic violence, coercion, or exploitation?
Was it cannabis-only, simple drug possession, or affected by Blake?
Do you need a firearm-rights restoration separately?
How old were you when the offense or case began?
Have you completed every part of the sentence, supervision, probation, parole, classes, or community service?
Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you currently have any pending criminal charge, open case, probation, parole, or other supervision?
Was this offense connected to your status as a human-trafficking or sex-trafficking survivor?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?
Where in Washington was the case handled?
What case, docket, cause, or arrest number appears on the record?

pathways.label: Adult misdemeanor / gross-misdemeanor vacation under RCW 9.96.060
pathways.summary: A person convicted of a misdemeanor or gross misdemeanor may apply to the sentencing court for vacation. The court has discretion if the applicant satisfies the statutory requirements. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060") General misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor requirements Sentence complete All sentence terms completed, including financial obligations, unless a special route applies Pending charges No pending criminal charges in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court Offense not violent Violent misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor offenses and attempts are barred
ruleClauses: A person convicted of a misdemeanor or gross misdemeanor may apply to the sentencing court for vacation. The court has discretion if the applicant satisfies the statutory requirements. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")

Sentence complete All sentence terms completed, including financial obligations, unless a special route applies
Pending charges No pending criminal charges in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court
Offense not violent Violent misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor offenses and attempts are barred

Waiting period - ordinary non-DV At least 3 years since the later of release from supervision/probation, release from confinement, or sentencing date
New convictions No new conviction in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court in the 3 years before application
Protection/restraining orders Current qualifying protection/no-contact/antiharassment/civil restraining order can block; recent violation can also block
Firearm rights Vacation does not automatically restore firearm rights

Wilma routing rule: If the user says
Wilma should check non-conviction deletion, not conviction vacation.
Misdemeanor / gross misdemeanor vacation

File the petition in the sentencing court using the CrRLJ 09.0100 petition and proposed CrRLJ 09.0200 order. Notice/local hearing practices vary; Washington LawHelp notes that if the prosecutor agrees, the person may not need a hearing, but local practices vary by county. (
"https://www.washingtonlawhelp.org/en/vacate-misdemeanor-conviction")

File in the sentencing superior court using CR 08.0900 and proposed CR 08.0920. Washington LawHelp notes that a person usually needs a Certificate of Discharge, must serve the prosecutor, and may need a hearing. ("https://www.washingtonlawhelp.org/en/vacate-felony-conviction")

Use the WSP non-conviction deletion request process for CHRI deletion under RCW 10.97.060. This is separate from a court vacate order. WSP also says dismissal/acquittal information is non-conviction and not disseminated to the public, but it remains available for criminal justice inquiries unless deleted under RCW 10.97.060 or by court order. ("https://wsp.wa.gov/crime/criminal-history/")

For misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor vacation, the clerk must immediately transmit the vacation order to WSP and local law enforcement; WSP/local agencies update records and send the order to the FBI. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")

pathways.label: Blake drug-possession vacation and refund route
pathways.summary: Washington has a special State v. Blake pathway for unconstitutional drug-possession convictions. Washington Courts provides Blake-specific motions and orders to vacate drug-possession convictions, refund paid legal financial obligations, waive unpaid balances, and handle full or partial case vacatur in superior, district, and municipal courts. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/documents/Blake%20001%20SupCt%20Blake%20Motion%20April%202026.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Blake order forms state that convictions based on RCW 69.50.4013 or RCW 69.50.4014, predecessor statutes, similar local ordinances, and certain inchoate offenses are…

ruleClauses: Washington has a special State v. Blake pathway for unconstitutional drug-possession convictions. Washington Courts provides Blake-specific motions and orders to vacate drug-possession convictions, refund paid legal financial obligations, waive unpaid balances, and handle full or partial case vacatur in superior, district, and municipal courts. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/documents/Blake%20001%20SupCt%20Blake%20Motion%20April%202026.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Blake order forms state that convictions based on RCW 69.50.4013 or RCW 69.50.4014, predecessor statutes, similar local ordinances, and certain inchoate offenses are unconstitutionally void and must be vacated, with LFOs and accrued interest paid pursuant to those convictions refunded. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/documents/Blake%20006%20Order_CLJ%20partial%20vacate.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
1 Was the conviction for simple possession of a controlled substance?
2 Was it under RCW 69.50.4013, RCW 69.50.4014, predecessor statutes, or similar local ordinance?
3 Was it before the Blake decision / under the unconstitutional possession law?
4 Were there other counts in the same case?
5 Did the user pay legal financial obligations?
6 Was the conviction already vacated?
7 Has the user applied for a Blake refund?

A person convicted of a misdemeanor cannabis offense who was 21 or older at the time of the offense may apply to the sentencing court for vacation. RCW 9.96.060 says misdemeanor cannabis offenses include RCW 69.50.4014 from July 1, 2004 onward, specified predecessor statutes, and equivalent municipal ordinances; if the applicant qualifies, the court shall vacate the record. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Wilma caution: Cannabis vacation is not the same as Blake drug-possession relief. Ask for the statute, date, age at offense, and whether it was cannabis-only.

pathways.label: Victim / survivor conviction-vacation route
pathways.summary: Washington has special vacation pathways when the conviction was committed as a result of being a victim of sex trafficking, prostitution, commercial sexual abuse of a minor, sexual assault, or domestic violence. For misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor convictions, RCW 9.96.080 allows the victim or prosecutor to apply to the sentencing court. The applicant must provide an affidavit proving by a preponderance of the evidence that the offense was committed as a result of the victimization; must have no pending charges other than prostitution; must meet new-conviction rules; and must satisfy victim-penalty-assessment/restitution requirements unless…
ruleClauses: Washington has special vacation pathways when the conviction was committed as a result of being a victim of sex trafficking, prostitution, commercial sexual abuse of a minor, sexual assault, or domestic violence.

For misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor convictions, RCW 9.96.080 allows the victim or prosecutor to apply to the sentencing court. The applicant must provide an affidavit proving by a preponderance of the evidence that the offense was committed as a result of the victimization; must have no pending charges other than prostitution; must meet new-conviction rules; and must satisfy victim-penalty-assessment/restitution requirements unless exceptions apply. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.080")

For Class B or Class C felony convictions, RCW 9.94A.648 allows a similar victim route. The applicant must prove the victimization connection by a preponderance of evidence, have no pending charges other than prostitution, meet 5-year or 10-year new-offense rules depending on Class C or Class B, pay the crime-victim penalty assessment, and pay applicable restitution; violent/person offense restrictions, felony DUI/physical-control exclusions, and promoting prostitution exclusions still apply. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94A.648")
Wilma trauma rule: Ask only what is needed to route:
Was this charge connected to trafficking, sexual assault, domestic violence, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or controlling what happened?
Do not ask for graphic details. Trigger manual/legal review.

pathways.label: Juvenile-record sealing under RCW 13.50.260
pathways.summary: Washington juvenile records use a separate sealing statute. Courts hold regular administrative sealing hearings and, at disposition, schedule a sealing hearing after the latest of the respondents 18th birthday, anticipated end of probation, or anticipated release/completion of parole from juvenile rehabilitation. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260") The court does not schedule administrative sealing if the adjudication was, at the time of offense, a most serious offense, a sex offense under chapter 9A.44, or a drug offense as defined in RCW 9.94A.030. At the administrative sealing hearing, the court seals if the…
ruleClauses: Washington juvenile records use a separate sealing statute. Courts hold regular administrative sealing hearings and, at disposition, schedule a sealing hearing after the latest of the respondents 18th birthday, anticipated end of probation, or anticipated release/completion of parole from juvenile rehabilitation. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

The court does not schedule administrative sealing if the adjudication was, at the time of offense, a most serious offense, a sex offense under chapter 9A.44, or a drug offense as defined in RCW 9.94A.030. At the administrative sealing hearing, the court seals if the respondent is no longer on supervision and has paid restitution owed to the individual victim, excluding restitution owed to insurers or health-care payors. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

If a juvenile case ends in acquittal after fact-finding or dismissal with prejudice, the court must immediately seal the official juvenile court record, subject to any state appeal rights. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

For motion-based juvenile sealing, Class A offenses require 5 consecutive crime-free years in the community, no pending juvenile/criminal proceeding, no pending diversion proceeding, no current sex-offender registration duty or relief from it, no rape 1, rape 2, or forcible-compulsion indecent liberties, and payment of restitution to the individual victim. Class B/C, gross misdemeanor, misdemeanor, and diversion records generally require 2 consecutive years in the community without conviction, no pending proceeding/diversion, no active registration duty, and payment of qualifying restitution. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

when.sourceConditionText: Adult Class A felony Usually not eligible for normal felony vacate Manual review only

Adult violent offense / crime against person Usually barred, with narrow exceptions Assault 2, certain Assault 3, and Robbery 2 may be eligible if no firearm/deadly weapon/sexual motivation enhancement
Adult DUI / physical control felony Barred from normal felony vacation RCW 9.94A.640 excludes felony DUI / physical control
Firearm rights issue Separate restoration process Vacation does not automatically restore firearm rights
Adult misdemeanor / gross misdemeanor conviction Petition and Declaration for Order Vacating Conviction RCW 9.96.060; usually 3-year wait, 5-year DV wait, special cannabis/victim routes
Adult felony conviction Motion and Declaration for Order Vacating Record of Felony Conviction RCW 9.94A.640; mostly Class B/C, with 10-year / 5-year rules
Dismissal / acquittal / no conviction WSP deletion of non-conviction data RCW 10.97.060; not the same as conviction vacation

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Vacation
Washington
vacation
means the court lets the person withdraw a guilty plea or sets aside the guilty verdict, dismisses the charging document, and vacates the judgment and sentence. For misdemeanors/gross misdemeanors, the court uses this mechanism under RCW 9.96.060; for felonies, it uses RCW 9.94A.640. (

"https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Effect of vacation
After felony vacation, the conviction is not included in criminal history for later sentencing purposes, the person is released from penalties/disabilities from the offense, and the person may state they were never convicted of that crime for employment applications. State Patrol and local law enforcement generally may not disseminate or disclose the vacated conviction except to criminal justice agencies. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94a.640")
For misdemeanors/gross misdemeanors, the person may answer employment or housing applications as if they were not convicted of that crime, and WSP/local law enforcement update their records and transmit the order to the FBI. But vacation does not automatically restore firearm rights. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Not full expungement
Wilma must not call adult Washington vacation
expungement
in the product. Washington LawHelp states directly that vacating a conviction does not make the court record private, does not remove the record from court indexes/public court websites, and does not delete adult conviction records entirely. ("https://www.washingtonlawhelp.org/en/vacate-misdemeanor-conviction")

The Washington Courts vacating/sealing forms page lists the cannabis, treaty-fishing, juvenile sealing, and felony vacation forms, and notes that local county superior court rules may require additional documents. (

"https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/?fa=forms.contribute&formID=38") The full Washington Courts forms list also identifies the misdemeanor vacation order and instructions, felony vacation forms, and juvenile sealing forms. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/?fa=forms.static&staticID=14")

sourceSections.title: Adult misdemeanor / gross misdemeanor vacation - RCW 9.96.060
sourceSections.text:
A person convicted of a misdemeanor or gross misdemeanor may apply to the sentencing court for vacation. The court has discretion if the applicant satisfies the statutory requirements. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
General misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor requirements
Wilma must confirm:

Requirement Washington rule
Sentence complete All sentence terms completed, including financial obligations, unless a special route applies
Pending charges No pending criminal charges in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court
Offense not violent Violent misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor offenses and attempts are barred
Offense not excluded sex/obscenity/child exploitation Chapter 9.68, 9.68A, and 9A.44 offenses are barred, except failure to register
Waiting period - ordinary non-DV At least 3 years since the later of release from supervision/probation, release from confinement, or sentencing date
New convictions No new conviction in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court in the 3 years before application
Protection/restraining orders Current qualifying protection/no-contact/antiharassment/civil restraining order can block; recent violation can also block
Firearm rights Vacation does not automatically restore firearm rights
RCW 9.96.060 lists the general bars: incomplete sentence, pending charges, violent offense, DUI/physical-control/prior-offense issues, sex/obscenity/child-exploitation categories, domestic-violence conditions, ordinary 3-year timing, new convictions in the prior 3 years, and qualifying protection/restraining-order issues. (

"https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Domestic violence misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor route
Domestic violence misdemeanors are not automatically barred, but they have stricter rules. Wilma must check: written notice to the prosecuting attorney, whether the person has two or more DV convictions from different incidents, whether they truthfully disclosed prior DV convictions, and whether at least 5 years have passed since completing original sentence conditions including treatment, excluding payment of financial obligations. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Wilma caution: DV cases are a manual-review category. A vacated DV conviction may help with federal firearms definition issues, but vacation alone still does not automatically restore Washington firearm rights. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
DUI /
prior offense
issue
RCW 9.96.060 excludes DUI, actual physical control, railroad intoxication, and certain
prior offense
cases where there has been a later alcohol/drug violation within 10 years or less than 10 years has passed since the prior-offense arrest. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Wilma caution: Ask if the conviction was DUI, physical control, reckless driving reduced from DUI, negligent driving 1st, or another alcohol/drug driving-related
prior offense.

sourceSections.title: Adult felony vacation - RCW 9.94A.640
sourceSections.text:
For felonies, the person generally must have been discharged under RCW 9.94A.637, then apply to the sentencing courtfor vacation. The court may clear the conviction by letting the person withdraw the guilty plea or setting aside the guilty verdict and dismissing the information or indictment. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94a.640")
Felony eligibility table

Felony category Washington rule
Class A felony Not available under normal RCW 9.94A.640 route
Class B felony 10-year wait from latest of release from community custody, release from confinement, or sentencing date; no new conviction in prior 10 years
Class C felony 5-year wait from latest of release from community custody, release from confinement, or sentencing date; no new conviction in prior 5 years
Pending charges Any pending criminal charge in Washington, another state, or federal court blocks
Violent offense / crime against persons Usually barred
Narrow violent/person exceptions Assault 2, certain Assault 3, and Robbery 2 may be vacated if no firearm, deadly weapon, or sexual motivation enhancement
Felony DUI / physical control Barred
Firearm rights Vacation does not restore firearm rights
RCW 9.94A.640 bars vacation if there are pending charges, if the offense was a violent offense or crime against persons except narrow listed offenses, if the Class B/Class C waiting and clean-period requirements are not met, or if the offense was felony DUI/physical control. (

"https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94a.640")
Wilma caution: Felony vacation requires offense class and exact RCW. Do not route based only on
felony
or
nonviolent
as described by the user.

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction deletion - RCW 10.97.060
sourceSections.text:
Washingtons actual
deletion
route is mainly for non-conviction data, not adult convictions. RCW 10.97.060 says non-conviction criminal-history record information is subject to deletion from generally searched criminal justice agency files when 2 years or more have passed since favorable disposition, or 3 years have passed from arrest/citation/warrant for an offense where no conviction was obtained, unless the person is a fugitive or the case remains under active prosecution. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=10.97.060")
The agency may refuse deletion if the disposition was a deferred prosecution or similar diversion, the person has a prior felony or gross misdemeanor conviction, or the person has been arrested for or charged with another crime during the intervening period. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=10.97.060")
Wilma routing rule: If the user says
charges were dismissed,

I was acquitted,

they never filed charges,
or
it was dropped,
Wilma should check non-conviction deletion, not conviction vacation.

sourceSections.title: Blake drug-possession vacation and refunds
sourceSections.text:
Washington has a special State v. Blake pathway for unconstitutional drug-possession convictions. Washington Courts provides Blake-specific motions and orders to vacate drug-possession convictions, refund paid legal financial obligations, waive unpaid balances, and handle full or partial case vacatur in superior, district, and municipal courts. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/documents/Blake%20001%20SupCt%20Blake%20Motion%20April%202026.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Blake order forms state that convictions based on RCW 69.50.4013 or RCW 69.50.4014, predecessor statutes, similar local ordinances, and certain inchoate offenses are unconstitutionally void and must be vacated, with LFOs and accrued interest paid pursuant to those convictions refunded. ("https://www.courts.wa.gov/forms/documents/Blake%20006%20Order_CLJ%20partial%20vacate.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma Blake questions:
1 Was the conviction for simple possession of a controlled substance?
2 Was it under RCW 69.50.4013, RCW 69.50.4014, predecessor statutes, or similar local ordinance?
3 Was it before the Blake decision / under the unconstitutional possession law?
4 Were there other counts in the same case?
5 Did the user pay legal financial obligations?
6 Was the conviction already vacated?
7 Has the user applied for a Blake refund?

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanor cannabis vacation
sourceSections.text:
A person convicted of a misdemeanor cannabis offense who was 21 or older at the time of the offense may apply to the sentencing court for vacation. RCW 9.96.060 says misdemeanor cannabis offenses include RCW 69.50.4014 from July 1, 2004 onward, specified predecessor statutes, and equivalent municipal ordinances; if the applicant qualifies, the court shall vacate the record. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
Wilma caution: Cannabis vacation is not the same as Blake drug-possession relief. Ask for the statute, date, age at offense, and whether it was cannabis-only.

sourceSections.title: Victim/survivor vacation routes
sourceSections.text:
Washington has special vacation pathways when the conviction was committed as a result of being a victim of sex trafficking, prostitution, commercial sexual abuse of a minor, sexual assault, or domestic violence.
For misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor convictions, RCW 9.96.080 allows the victim or prosecutor to apply to the sentencing court. The applicant must provide an affidavit proving by a preponderance of the evidence that the offense was committed as a result of the victimization; must have no pending charges other than prostitution; must meet new-conviction rules; and must satisfy victim-penalty-assessment/restitution requirements unless exceptions apply. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.080")
For Class B or Class C felony convictions, RCW 9.94A.648 allows a similar victim route. The applicant must prove the victimization connection by a preponderance of evidence, have no pending charges other than prostitution, meet 5-year or 10-year new-offense rules depending on Class C or Class B, pay the crime-victim penalty assessment, and pay applicable restitution; violent/person offense restrictions, felony DUI/physical-control exclusions, and promoting prostitution exclusions still apply. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94A.648")
Wilma trauma rule: Ask only what is needed to route:
Was this charge connected to trafficking, sexual assault, domestic violence, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or controlling what happened?
Do not ask for graphic details. Trigger manual/legal review.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile record sealing - RCW 13.50.260
sourceSections.text:
Washington juvenile records use a separate sealing statute. Courts hold regular administrative sealing hearings and, at disposition, schedule a sealing hearing after the latest of the respondents 18th birthday, anticipated end of probation, or anticipated release/completion of parole from juvenile rehabilitation. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")
The court does not schedule administrative sealing if the adjudication was, at the time of offense, a most serious offense, a sex offense under chapter 9A.44, or a drug offense as defined in RCW 9.94A.030. At the administrative sealing hearing, the court seals if the respondent is no longer on supervision and has paid restitution owed to the individual victim, excluding restitution owed to insurers or health-care payors. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")
If a juvenile case ends in acquittal after fact-finding or dismissal with prejudice, the court must immediately seal the official juvenile court record, subject to any state appeal rights. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")
For motion-based juvenile sealing, Class A offenses require 5 consecutive crime-free years in the community, no pending juvenile/criminal proceeding, no pending diversion proceeding, no current sex-offender registration duty or relief from it, no rape 1, rape 2, or forcible-compulsion indecent liberties, and payment of restitution to the individual victim. Class B/C, gross misdemeanor, misdemeanor, and diversion records generally require 2 consecutive years in the community without conviction, no pending proceeding/diversion, no active registration duty, and payment of qualifying restitution. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

sourceSections.title: Waiting-period table
sourceSections.text:
Route Waiting / timing
Ordinary misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor vacation 3 years from later of release from supervision/probation, release from confinement, or sentencing date
DV misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor vacation 5 years after completing original sentence conditions, including treatment, excluding financial obligations
Misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor new-conviction lookback No new conviction in prior 3 years
Felony Class B vacation 10 years from latest release/sentencing trigger, and no new conviction in prior 10 years
Felony Class C vacation 5 years from latest release/sentencing trigger, and no new conviction in prior 5 years
Non-conviction deletion after favorable disposition 2 years after favorable disposition
Non-conviction deletion where no conviction obtained 3 years from arrest/citation/warrant, unless fugitive or active prosecution
Misdemeanor cannabis vacation No ordinary wait stated in the cannabis subsection if qualifying
Blake drug-possession vacatur Not a normal waiting-period route; eligibility is based on void conviction category
Juvenile administrative sealing Scheduled after age 18 / end probation / release or parole completion, whichever is latest
Juvenile Class A motion sealing 5 consecutive years in community, plus other requirements
Juvenile Class B/C/gross misdemeanor/misdemeanor/diversion sealing 2 consecutive years in community, plus other requirements

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
Misdemeanor / gross misdemeanor vacation
File the petition in the sentencing court using the CrRLJ 09.0100 petition and proposed CrRLJ 09.0200 order. Notice/local hearing practices vary; Washington LawHelp notes that if the prosecutor agrees, the person may not need a hearing, but local practices vary by county. (

"https://www.washingtonlawhelp.org/en/vacate-misdemeanor-conviction")
Felony vacation
File in the sentencing superior court using CR 08.0900 and proposed CR 08.0920. Washington LawHelp notes that a person usually needs a Certificate of Discharge, must serve the prosecutor, and may need a hearing. ("https://www.washingtonlawhelp.org/en/vacate-felony-conviction")
Non-conviction deletion
Use the WSP non-conviction deletion request process for CHRI deletion under RCW 10.97.060. This is separate from a court vacate order. WSP also says dismissal/acquittal information is non-conviction and not disseminated to the public, but it remains available for criminal justice inquiries unless deleted under RCW 10.97.060 or by court order. ("https://wsp.wa.gov/crime/criminal-history/")
After order is granted
For misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor vacation, the clerk must immediately transmit the vacation order to WSP and local law enforcement; WSP/local agencies update records and send the order to the FBI. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")

sourceSections.title: Record request process
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should start with the Washington State Patrol criminal-history record and the court docket. WSP says Washington criminal-history records consist of fingerprint-based records and disposition information submitted by law enforcement agencies and courts. Public requests are generally limited to conviction information, arrests less than one year old with pending dispositions, and sex/kidnapping-offender registration information. ("https://wsp.wa.gov/crime/criminal-history/")
WSP options and fees:

Request type Fee / note
WATCH online name/DOB check $11, immediate online result
Mail conviction CHRI request $32
Fingerprint card CHRI request $58
Notarized letter Additional $15
Non-conviction CHRI copy Fingerprint card + $12
In-person non-conviction record review No fee; fingerprints required; limited inspection time
WSP lists the WATCH, mail, in-person, fingerprint, notarized-letter, non-conviction CHRI, and record-review options and fees. (

"https://wsp.wa.gov/crime/criminal-history/")

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake questions for Washington
sourceSections.text:
Ask in this order:
1
Was this an adult case or juvenile case?

2
Was it a conviction, dismissal, acquittal, no-charge arrest, diversion/deferred prosecution, or still pending?

3
What court handled it: district, municipal, superior, or juvenile?

4
What county and case number?

5
What was the exact charge and RCW/statute?

6
Was it a misdemeanor, gross misdemeanor, Class B felony, Class C felony, or something else?

7
When were you sentenced?

8
When were you released from jail/prison/confinement?

9
When did probation/supervision/community custody end?

10
Are all sentence terms complete, including treatment and required obligations?

11
Do you owe restitution to an individual victim?

12
Do you have any pending charges in Washington, another state, federal court, or tribal court?

13
Have you had any new convictions in the relevant 3-, 5-, or 10-year period?

14
Was the case DUI, physical control, reckless/negligent driving connected to alcohol/drugs, or another prior offense?

15
Was the offense domestic violence, sex-related, violent, crime against persons, child exploitation, obscenity/pornography, firearm/deadly weapon-enhanced, or sexually motivated?

16
Was this conviction connected to trafficking, sexual assault, domestic violence, coercion, or exploitation?

17
Was it cannabis-only, simple drug possession, or affected by Blake?

18
Do you need a firearm-rights restoration separately?

15. Wilma
do not say eligible unless&
rules
Wilma should not say
you are eligible
unless all required facts are confirmed.
For ordinary misdemeanor/gross misdemeanor vacation, confirm the offense is not excluded, sentence terms are complete, no pending charges exist, the correct 3-year or 5-year wait has passed, no disqualifying new convictions exist, and protection-order restrictions do not block relief. ("https://apps.leg.wa.gov/Rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.96.060")
For felony vacation, confirm discharge status, felony class, no pending charges, no barred violent/person/DUI category, the 5- or 10-year wait has passed, and the clean-period requirement is met. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=9.94a.640")
For non-conviction deletion, confirm the case is truly non-conviction data, the 2- or 3-year period applies, the person is not a fugitive, the case is not under active prosecution, and no agency-refusal factor applies. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=10.97.060")
For juvenile sealing, confirm offense category, age/timing, supervision status, restitution to individual victim, pending proceedings, sex-offender-registration status, and whether the case should have been administratively sealed. ("https://app.leg.wa.gov/rcw/default.aspx?cite=13.50.260")

sourceSections.title: Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Washington State, train Wilma with this core logic:
Washington adult convictions are usually
vacated,
not expunged.Vacation helps WSP/local dissemination and lets the person say they were not convicted of that offense, but court records and private/FBI records may still show information.Misdemeanors/gross misdemeanors route through RCW 9.96.060; ordinary wait is 3 years, DV wait is 5 years.Felonies route through RCW 9.94A.640; Class B is 10 years, Class C is 5 years, and Class A is not the normal route.Non-convictions route through RCW 10.97.060 deletion, not vacation.Blake drug-possession cases have their own vacate/refund workflow.Juvenile cases route through RCW 13.50.260 sealing.Never promise deletion, firearm-right restoration, immigration safety, licensing safety, or FBI/private-background-check removal from a Washington vacate order.
